course_description:
Overview of the contemporary global publishing industry and book culture, with a particular emphasis on the Australian publishing industry.

This capstone course examines a range of contemporary issues shaping writing and communication practices across diverse industries and contexts.

Additional timetable information
Whilst every effort is made to place students in their preferred activity, it is not always possible for a student to be enrolled in their tutorial of choice. If you require assistance, please ensure that you email
timetabling.commarts@enquire.uq.edu.au
from your
UQ student email
with:
Your name
Your student ID
The course code
A list of three tutorial preferences (in order of preference)
Reason for the change – e.g. timetable clash, elite athlete status, SAP
Teaching staff do not have access to change tutorials or help with timetables; all timetabling changes must be processed through the Timetabling Team.
course_aims: This course introduces students to the publishing industry and to writing and editing practices across a range of professional contexts, including publishing and communications. The course is delivered through seminars, readings and, with a program of guest writers, editors, and publishing professionals.

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate an understanding of contemporary writing, editing, and publishing‑adjacent industries, including professional communities, events, and career pathways in Australian and global contexts.
Identify and evaluate strategies for professional engagement and career development, including networking, conference participation, and the use of social media and other industry platforms.
Apply critical reading and reviewing practices to produce audience‑appropriate responses to published texts, including short‑form reviews for public or professional contexts.
Research and synthesise information about industry practices, debates, or professional trends, and communicate findings clearly to a specialist or peer audience.
Communicate professionally and effectively in oral, written, and visual formats, including participation in scholarly or industry‑style forums such as symposia and poster presentations.

assessment_details.title: Assessment 1 - Video Book Review
assessment_details.due_date: 23/03/2026 4:00 pm Submit written materials in one file via Turnitin through Blackboard. Submit video materials in one file to Echo360 through Blackboard.
assessment_details.task_description: In this assessment, students will create a short‑form digital book review designed for contemporary online audiences. Students will select a book of any genre published in 2025 or 2026 and produce a two‑minute TikTok‑style video review , supported by a written script and planning documentation (to be discussed in seminars). The project requires students to demonstrate critical engagement with the chosen text while adapting their review to the conventions, affordances and audience expectations of a specific digital platform and audience. Accompanying planning materials will outline the intended platform for publication, target audience, content strategy, hashtags, captions, links, and any relevant promotional or contextual considerations. This assessment develops students’ skills in critical reading, audience‑aware writing, digital storytelling and professional communication, and reflects contemporary practices in book reviewing, promotion, and cultural commentary. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
TurnItIn Receipts:
This assignment will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using TurnItIn . Before submitting any assignments for this course you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
When you successfully submit your assessment to TurnItIn you will see text confirming your submission is complete, before being redirected to your Assignment inbox. On this page you can:
View the name of the submitted file View date and time of the upload Resubmit your paper (if necessary) Download your submitted paper Download digital receipt.
If you cannot see your submission in your Assignment inbox you should regard your submission as unsuccessful. Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your TurnItIn assignment .
EchoVideo Submission
This assignment will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using EchoVideo . Before submitting any assignments for this course, you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your EchoVideo .
When you successfully submit your assessment to EchoVideo, you will see text confirming your submission is complete. You will also receive an email confirmation message.
Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Assessment 2 - WEP Symposium poster and presentation
assessment_details.category: Practical/ Demonstration, Poster, Role play/ Simulation
assessment_details.due_date: 19/05/2026 - 26/05/2026 Students will be allocated to presentation groups during the semester . Groups determine the week in which each student will present their poster as part of the symposium. Attendance at both symposium weeks is required. All students must: Present their poster in their allocated presentation week, and Attend and participate in the other symposium week as an audience member and peer assessor. Attendance at both scheduled symposium sessions—the session in which you present and the session in which you act as a peer assessor —is mandatory in order to receive a grade for this assessment. The symposium dates are published in the ECP; students must ensure they are available and make the necessary arrangements to attend.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessment factor. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment Overview This assessment requires students to produce a professional poster and deliver an oral presentation based on the recommendations developed in their industry report (assessment three) . The assessment will be conducted during a scheduled symposium , designed to simulate professional and industry-facing presentation contexts such as conferences, briefings, or stakeholder discussions. This assessment is classified as a secure assessment . Students are required to be present at the symposium class and to demonstrate their own understanding of the work by presenting their ideas, responding to questions, and participating in discussion of classmate's presentations. Students will be allocated to presentation groups during the semester . Groups determine the week in which each student will present their poster as part of the symposium. Attendance at both symposium weeks is required. All students must: Present their poster in their allocated presentation week, and Attend and participate in the other symposium week as an audience member and peer assessor. Both presentation and participation form part of the assessment requirements. Failure to attend either week may impact assessment outcomes and will be managed in accordance with University policies on attendance and deferred assessment. Poster Students will design and produce a poster that communicates: The issue, challenge, or focus area developed in their report Key insights or findings relevant to that issue Clear, well-articulated recommendations for practice, change, or career development The poster should be professionally presented and accessible to a broad academic or industry-informed audience. Students are expected to translate their written work into concise, visually organised form suitable for discussion and engagement. Details regarding poster size, format, and presentation logistics will be provided during the semester. Oral Presentation and Discussion Students will deliver an oral presentation based on their poster and report recommendations. Rather than focusing on length, the emphasis of the presentation is on students’ ability to: Clearly explain the focus of their work Justify and articulate their recommendations Demonstrate understanding of the research and thinking underpinning those recommendations Engage professionally in discussion and respond to questions Delivering approripate feedback to peers The presentation and discussion component is central to the secure nature of the assessment, as it requires students to demonstrate preparedness, originality, and ownership of their work. This presentation may be recorded for grade moderation purposes. Recordings will be kept securely according to university policies. Peer Assessment Component This assessment includes a peer assessment component conducted during the symposium day. Students will be required to engage with the work of other presenters and provide structured feedback using a supplied online form. Peer assessment is designed to support: Critical engagement with classmates’ work Reflection on effective communication and professional practice The development of evaluative judgement Peer feedback will be criteria‑guided and focused on the clarity, persuasiveness, and credibility of each presenter’s recommendations. Peer assessment will be used alongside staff assessment and may be moderated to ensure fairness and consistency. Participation in the peer assessment process is a required part of the symposium activity. Relationship to Other Assessments and Feedback The poster and presentation function as a formative stage for the final industry report. Feedback generated through audience questions and discussion on the symposium day can be used to inform revisions to the report, including the clarification, strengthening, or reconsideration of recommendations where appropriate. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Assessment 3 - Portfolio and report
assessment_details.weight: 50% 10 portfolio items and a report (2000-3000 words)
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment Overview This assessment consists of a portfolio of work developed across the semester and a formal industry report . The portfolio is intentionally open in form and content and is designed to support students working towards a wide range of professional outcomes. Part A: Portfolio (Ten Pieces) Students will submit ten portfolio pieces developed over the semester. Portfolio pieces are developed through weekly in‑class tasks , which may be submitted, revised, or adapted to support the final report. Portfolio pieces may take a wide range of forms, depending on the student’s professional interests and these will be discussed in seminars. The portfolio is intended to demonstrate process, development, and professional thinking , rather than polish alone. A minimum of 8 pieces need to be submitted to receive a pass or above for this task. If less than 8 pieces are submitted, the portfolio component of this assessment will receive an automatic grade of 3 (marginal fail) or below. Part B: Industry Report (2,000–3,000 words) Students will submit a formal report that situates their portfolio work within a broader professional or industry context. The report may take one of two approaches. Option 1: Career Pathways Report Students identify their intended professional area and use the report to analyse: Employment and career pathways relevant to their interests Opportunities for professional development and progression Community building, networks, and professional cultures Visibility, positioning, and self-presentation within the field Structural, ethical, or practical challenges in the industry Short- and medium-term professional goals The report should include practical recommendations the student can apply to their own portfolio, professional development, and career planning. Option 2: Industry Issue Analysis and Recommendations Students identify a key issue, challenge, or ongoing conversation within their chosen professional context and: Analyse the issue in relation to industry structures or practices Consider impacts on workers, organisations, or communities Engage with relevant research, policy, or professional discourse Make clear, realistic recommendations for change or improvement Recommendations may be directed towards organisations, employers, industry bodies, or practitioners and should demonstrate critical and ethical awareness. Format and Presentation The portfolio is open in format . You may submit their portfolio in a range of forms appropriate to the range of materials as well as your professional goals and areas of interest. Possible formats include (but are not limited to) a compiled document, a digital or web‑based portfolio, or a curated collection of materials presented in another suitable medium. The specific mode of submission for the portfolio will be discussed with, and approved by, the lecturer to ensure it is appropriate, accessible, and assessable. The report is a professional document . It should be clearly structured, written in an appropriate academic or professional register, and presented in a conventional written format (e.g. a structured report or essay-style document). The report must meet standard expectations for clarity, coherence, referencing, and professional presentation. It follows on from your Symposium Poster assessment , and it is suggested that you use feedback received on the day to improve your report. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_activities.topic: Week 1 Introduction to the course, assessment structure, and expectations for the semester. Discussion of texts, professional writing contexts, and industry conversations. Please bring a copy of a favourite book and Yellowface . Complete the readings in the Week 1 resources folder. Learning outcomes: L01, L02
Week 2 Book reviews & book review culture.
Week 3: Work on your videos No class: work on your book review videos Learning outcomes: L01, L02
Week 4 The portfolio career of the writer and editor. Learning outcomes: L01, L04
Week 5 Challenges and opportunities in writing‑related industries. Learning outcomes: L01, L02
Week 6 Physical formats, production processes, and commercial considerations relevant to professional writing contexts. Learning outcomes: L01, L02
Week 7 Community building in the writing world. Learning outcomes: L01, L04
Week 8 Promotional strategies, audience development, and market positioning for writers and communicators. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04
Week 9 Australian writing and content within international markets, platforms, and cultural contexts. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04
Week 10 How written work is sold, commissioned, distributed, and sustained across a variety of professional contexts. Learning outcomes: L01, L04
Week 11 Reflection, professional identity, and future pathways
Week 12 In-class symposium and presentations tonight.
Week 13 In-class symposium and presentations tonight.

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . Course guidelines Communication Expectations While you are a student at UQ, all communication must be conducted according to the UQ Student Code of Conduct . The UQ Library has a helpful Communicate and collaborate online module . Email is the primary way for you to send messages to, and receive information from, the School and our staff. You must use your UQ email address (not a private address) to communicate with staff. You should add a clear subject line, including course code, and a 2-3 word statement. You can send email at any time, however please do not expect responses outside normal working hours (Monday to Friday from ~8am to ~5pm). Emails that constitute bullying, harassment or discrimination against staff contravene the Student Code of Conduct. Emails like this will be reported to the University, and the matter will be pursued as misconduct. Learning activities
